issue_title: Clarify "What exactly are you trying to pull?"
issue_body:
https://github.com/uiri/toml/blob/df2b2df8a1101999b0b7dd7db6a41afa6ad4c675/toml.py#L61-L62

```python
    if not isinstance(s, basestring):
        raise TypeError("What exactly are you trying to pull?")
```

It's not a very useful error message if you have to dig into the source to figure out what it means. In this case, a `bytes` was being passed in, which is not permissible in python 3. Better would be to avoid `basestring` at all, as it is basically nonsense. Check for `bytes` or `unicode` and encode or decode appropriately.

issue_title: New error when mixing gl and gles on web service
issue_body:
I am working with an open source project called [lovr](https://github.com/bjornbytes/lovr) which [uses glad](https://github.com/bjornbytes/lovr/tree/master/src/lib/glad). Its current GLAD generation link is [this URL](http://glad.dav1d.de/#profile=core&language=c&specification=gl&api=gl%3D3.3&api=gles2%3D3.0&extensions=GL_ARB_texture_storage&extensions=GL_EXT_texture_compression_s3tc&extensions=GL_EXT_texture_filter_anisotropic&extensions=GL_EXT_texture_sRGB&extensions=GL_OVR_multiview
).

Sometime in the last week or three, this URL stopped working. Attempting to generate a GLAD from this URL on the web service now results in a message

> Warnings/Errors:
> Core profile not available when selecting any gles API, automatically changed

It then generates a Compatibility profile.

When I spoke to the lovr developer, he explained that the goal was to get a Core profile when running with plain GL and whatever profile when running with gles2. This is because the code targets both desktops and on emscripten, and Desktop-OpenGL Core is more similar to gles2 than OpenGL Compatibility is.

There are two issues here:
- What would be the correct way for lovr to get its gl core + gles2-supporting headers? I don't know what it was the web service was doing before when it simply allowed "gles2 core", but it *worked*.
- *Even if what Lovr is doing is incorrect*, it is problematic for there to be URLs which previously worked on the web service but now no longer work. I maintain a fork of Lovr where I use additional extensions that Lovr master does not use. Previously I could add or remove extensions by loading Lovr's URL up and changing the extensions list to add or remove one or two extensions. However now running the URL produces a compatibility profile, which is not what Lovr targets. In other words, removing support for core+gles outputs means third party consumers of older GLAD-using projects are "stuck"— I cannot necessarily patch Lovr to migrate it away from "core+gles2" because I do not directly control the original project, but I can't generate a new core+gles2 source file either.

I am not certain what my "expected behavior" here is, but: Would it be possible to alter the web service so that generating the "illegal" core+gles2 profile either becomes a *warning* (ie emits the illegal profile rather than silently altering it to compatibility) or provides more useful advice about what to do?

Thanks

In my opinion, and probably in most people's opinion, this should not be considered an antipattern or be enabled by default. Most plugin authors prefer to use implicit scope, at least for local variables inside functions. When scope is specified everywhere, the code inside functions becomes less readable. It also trains the reader to ignore the scope, thereby not noticing when a special is used.

Even inside one's main config file, it would be strange and unnecessary to specify global scope for every variable you set outside of a function.

issue_title: git checkout new branch suggestion improvement
issue_body:
Currently if i do the following:

    > git checkout my-new-branch
     error: pathspec 'my-new-branch' did not match any file(s) known to git.
    > fuck
I get the following suggestion:

    > git branch my-new-branch && git checkout my-new-branch

This works fine, but what i more likely meant (and this does the same thing) is:

    > git checkout -b my-new-branch

This is much more succinct and should probably be the suggestion in my opinion.

issue_title: User should be able to minimise verbosity of cuppa output
issue_body:
It should be possible to control the verbosity of cuppa's output for example by handling cuppa specific flags such as `--cuppa-quiet` and also honour scons-wide options like `-s, --silent, --quiet`.

Implementing this would require the addition of a cuppa 'logging' facility so that all components of cuppa printed console output through this facility. This may also allow the centralisation of the colourisation facility making its use more uniform and simpler throughout the code-base.

issue_body:
Hi.  Trying to have python determine the best active directory controller to connect to.  I'm connecting to the AD configuration partition and searching the subnet objects.  Everything in the ldap3 library's been working great but seems that one particular value isn't coming through when I query 'subnet' objects.  I think it's because it's a backlink or reference to another object that isn't being handled correctly.

Verified today with 2.1.1 and latest 2.2.1

I can see the siteObject value just find using ldapsearch as well as other command line tools:

    using ldapsearch

    ldapsearch -x -h mycontroller.mydomain.com -b "CN=Subnets,CN=Sites,CN=Configuration,dc=mydomain,dc=com" -D "ldapaccount@mydomain.com" -W "(cn=10.20.0.0/16)" siteObject

    **Output:**
    Enter LDAP Password: *********
    # extended LDIF
    #
    # LDAPv3
    # base <CN=Subnets,CN=Sites,CN=Configuration,dc=mydomain,dc=com> with scope subtree
    # filter: (cn=10.20.0.0/16)

    # 10.20.0.0/16, Subnets, Sites, Configuration, mydomain.com
    dn: CN=10.20.0.0/16,CN=Subnets,CN=Sites,CN=Configuration,DC=mydomain,DC=com

    # search result

    # numResponses: 2

The output above shows siteObject coming back with the DN of the site I'm looking for...

Same goes for other tools using the same account, so not a permission issue.  However, when using ldap3 and asking for all attributes '*' siteObject isn't returned at all.

    {'uSNCreated': 11713, 'dSCorePropagationData': [datetime.datetime(1601, 1, 1, 0, 0, tzinfo=OffsetTzInfo(offset=0, name='UTC'))], 'cn': '10.20.0.0/16', 'description': ['My site location'], 'whenChanged': datetime.datetime(2017, 1, 18, 18, 11, 15, tzinfo=OffsetTzInfo(offset=0, name='UTC')), 'objectGUID': 'xxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxx', 'objectClass': ['top', 'subnet'], 'name': '10.20.0.0/16', 'whenCreated': datetime.datetime(2009, 4, 2, 11, 23, 21, tzinfo=OffsetTzInfo(offset=0, name='UTC')), 'uSNChanged': 7401058, 'instanceType': 4, 'objectCategory': 'CN=Subnet,CN=Schema,CN=Configuration,DC=mydomain,DC=com', 'distinguishedName': 'CN=10.20.0.0/16,CN=Subnets,CN=Sites,CN=Configuration,DC=mydomain,DC=com'}

If I ask for just 3 attributes (cn,distinguishedname, and siteObject) I get an empty array for siteObject.

issue_title: How to manually add features and relations?
issue_body:
Thanks again for `gffutils`. It's a lifesaver.

I'm detecting *de novo* exons based on junction reads and want to add them to a `gffutils.FeatureDB`with a specific ID (`exon:chrom:start-stop:strand`) but they're getting added with an auto-named id `exon_1` rather than the one I gave it.

[Here](https://github.com/YeoLab/outrigger/commit/d45338b97dc2fb96b7e8297e5ec5923a533e576c#diff-0573fcefb6ee0c7b63d9aec6e2ebf770R124) is the commit I'm talking about.

So, even though I create features this way:

        exon_id = 'exon:{chrom}:{start}-{stop}:'.format(
            chrom=chrom, start=start, stop=stop)
        de_novo_exons = [gffutils.Feature(chrom, source='outrigger_de_novo',
                                     featuretype='exon', start=start, end=stop,
                                     strand=g.strand, id=exon_id + g.strand,
                                     attributes=dict(g.attributes.items()))
                         for g in overlapping_genes]

When I print the exon id, I get `exon_1` instead of `exon:chr10:128491290-128491347:-`.

```
progress('\tFound a novel exon ({}) in the gene {} '
                     '({})'.format(exon.id, gene.id,
                                   ','.join(gene.attributes['gene_name'])))
```

```
2016-08-17 13:43:47	Detecting de novo exons based on gaps between junctions ...
2016-08-17 13:43:47	Found new exon between outrigger.Region (junction:chr10:128491034-128491289:-) and outrigger.Region (junction:chr10:128491348-128492058:-)
2016-08-17 13:43:47		Found novel exon at exon:chr10:128491290-128491347:
2016-08-17 13:43:47		Found a novel exon (exon_1) in the gene ENSMUSG00000090841.1 (Myl6)
```

Reading the documentation for `gffutils.Feature`, I see that the ID is ignored if it's not from a database ...

```
id : None or string
    Database-specific primary key for this feature.  The only time this
    should not be None is if this feature is coming from a database, in
    which case it will be filled in automatically.
```

So is the solution here to aggregate all the exons I want to add, create a separate database with all the novel exons, then do `db.update(de_novo_exon_db)` once it's all ready? That seems clunky. Is there a way to override and force usage of the feature ID provided in the construction of a `gffutils.Feature` object?

issue_title: Train a lighter model also for profile views
issue_body:
Hi Davis, my congratulations for this amazing library.
I'm currently developing a project which should run on a constrained environment (Android) based on dlib-android, and I'm looking for ways to obtain a model with **fewer landmarks** (hoping it would speed up the detections) but most importantly which can recognize poses at the **full profile angle** of a single person in nearly ideal illumination setup.

I guess that iBUG database does not contain profile images (please tell me if I'm wrong), therefore I've been searching for a suitable dataset which I found could be [BioID](https://www.bioid.com/About/BioID-Face-Database) which gives me all the needed poses and 20 labeled landmarks. (But feel free to suggest some more prolific alternatives!)

My question is, is it possible to train on that dataset with dlib's trainer? How much in time and cost would it take (considering BioID has 1521 images, which probably I should mirror to have enough of them)? Would it be overkill to try to translate the data into the xmls required by your trainer?
_Consider I can use a fairly powerful cluster here at my University with 14 'blades' DELL PowerEdge M600 each with:
2 quad core Intel Xeon E5450 (12MB Cache, 3.00 GHz)
16 GB RAM
2 Hard disk 72GB in RAID-1 (mirroring)_

Also, I'm following with great interest @e-fominov pull request which is way cool.

Thanks, and sorry for asking such 'challenging' questions but that's for the sake of my MSc thesis :))

Hi sir, I would like to know if you have any suggestion on how to increase the speed of this example program for landmark detection in high quality images? I plan to utilize this library for mobile phones and performance would be a key issue in this case.

Thank you very much!

issue_title: 特性请求：并行 上传/下载
issue_body:
我的思路：
1. 大文件（大于10MiB）：
        利用request header - range对文件切片
        经过测试单文件最大可以有8个线程同时下载(UserAgent似乎需要为"netdisk")
        单线程最大下载速度2MiB/s
        多线程并发单文件最大下载速度8MiB/s

"""
ply

    mesh = trimesh.load('outdata.ply')
  File "c:\python27\lib\site-packages\trimesh\io\load.py", line 86, in load

issue_title: Comply with E731: do not assign a lambda expression, use a def
issue_body:
[migrate.py:206:1](https://github.com/kofrasa/migrate/blob/0.3.7/migrate.py#L205): `exec_sqlite3 = lambda a, b, c, d: exec_mysql(a, b, None, d)`
According to PEP8 this is an anti pattern.

See [Programming Recommendations](http://legacy.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0008/#programming-recommendations):

> Always use a def statement instead of an assignment statement that binds a lambda expression directly to an identifier.
> 
> Yes:
> 
> `def f(x): return 2*x`
> 
> No:
> 
> `f = lambda x: 2*x`
> 
> The first form means that the name of the resulting function object is specifically 'f' instead of the generic '<lambda>'. This is more useful for tracebacks and string representations in general. The use of the assignment statement eliminates the sole benefit a lambda expression can offer over an explicit def statement (i.e. that it can be embedded inside a larger expression)

Also, is it even being used? I couldn't find any reference to `exec_sqlite3` in the code at all.

issue_title: use nodes instead of movetime to limit analysis
issue_body:
```
go nodes 1000000
```

Actually we're limiting on movetime, and adjusting movetime based on nps. It seems like what we really want is ensure a certain number of nodes are crunched for each moves, and since version 7 stockfish allows that.

Benefits:

- No need to run initial benchmark!
- No need to adjust movetime!
- Clients can run with a Nice value, it doesn't matter that stockfish gets a full core. If the computer is running something else, the current move will take longer to analyse, but the quality remains the same.

Cons:

- ?

issue_title: Does freezegun support threading.Timer and concurrent.futures.Future triggers and timeouts?
issue_body:
We've been mocking time in Windows to coordinate datetime.now, time.sleep, threading.Timer triggers and concurrent.futures.Future timeouts. None of it works on Linux--likely due to different internals that don't trigger the mocks. People bring up freezegun, but we wanted to check if that's viable for handling this stuff.

I already see that time.sleep() doesn't get mocked by default, but we can route that ourselves. However, I am wondering about the rest. If freezegun is managing time, will it:

1. Start a function scheduled by threading.Timer when its delay is reached?
2. Time out a Future.result() call if the timeout threshold is reached?

issue_title: BrokenPipeError (?) when idle for a long time
issue_body:
Hi and thank you for this project!

When mpd is idle (after calling `client.stop()`) for a couple of hours, I cannot successfully call `client.play()` again. I am aware of ConnectionErrors that can occur so I catch them in my script. In this case, however, I get a BrokenPipeError (sometimes I don't get an error at all, but nevertheless mpd won't start to play reproducibly).

I wrote a test script (find it here: https://gist.github.com/speendo/0d9c1a028d045de3f7a6) to track the error down.

I ran this script on my raspberry pi with `nohup python3 test_mpd.py &`.

Here's a traceback of the Error (found in nohup.out):

    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "test_mpd.py", line 62, in <module>
        client.play()
      File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/dist-packages/mpd.py", line 600, in decorator
        return wrapper(self, name, args, bound_decorator(self, returnValue))
      File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/dist-packages/mpd.py", line 236, in _execute
        self._write_command(command, args)
      File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/dist-packages/mpd.py", line 263, in _write_command
        self._write_line(" ".join(parts))
      File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/dist-packages/mpd.py", line 243, in _write_line
        self._wfile.flush()
      File "/usr/lib/python3.4/socket.py", line 391, in write
        return self._sock.send(b)
    BrokenPipeError: [Errno 32] Broken pipe

And here's the log:

    2015-12-15 08:35:26,497 INFO: Log started
    2015-12-15 08:35:26,500 INFO: Attempt to (re-)connect to mpd server
    2015-12-15 08:35:26,503 INFO: Calling MPD connect('localhost', 6600, timeout=None)
    2015-12-15 08:35:26,566 INFO: Finished attempt to (re-)connect to mpd server
    2015-12-15 08:35:26,568 INFO: <bound method MPDClient.decorator of <mpd.MPDClient object at 0xb6a38590>>
    2015-12-15 08:35:26,570 INFO: Add playlist entry
    2015-12-15 08:35:26,573 DEBUG: Calling MPD clear()
    2015-12-15 08:35:26,578 DEBUG: Calling MPD add('http://uwstream2.somafm.com:9016',)
    2015-12-15 08:35:26,582 INFO: Start playing for the first time
    2015-12-15 08:35:26,585 DEBUG: Calling MPD play()
    2015-12-15 08:35:36,597 INFO: This obviously works. Switching off now for 4 hours.

    2015-12-15 12:35:36,725 INFO: Trying to switch on again now

I only have one monitor so when I'm listening to music with a bot, or need to do anything with a bot, and I'm playing a game, I have to alt tab out, send the command, then alt tab back in. Any help would be appreciated!

issue_title: Feature request: config option to change default link
issue_body:
I'm using `link: true` almost always, so I would rather be able to set it once.

Thanks!

First of all: Thank you very much for dotdrop!

To make it really usable for me, I need access to environment variables inside the templates.

That way - instead af having `{%@@ if profile == "PROFILENAME" @@%}` all over the place - I can just say `{{@@ env['MY_VAR'] @@}}` to set host-specific properties. I have an `.env`-file in my directory, that is not tracked by git and I invoke dotdrop with the following alias:

```
alias dotdrop='eval $(cat ~/dotfiles/.env | egrep -v "^#") ~/dotfiles/dotdrop.sh'
```

This also gives me the possibility to use dotdrop for files that need plain-text credentials or other information I don't want to share with the public.

You can see how I use it in my [dotfiles](https://github.com/open-dynaMIX/dotfiles) (beware: I should spend some time to clean it up a bit :wink:).

Since it's just two lines, that needs to be changed, for the time being, I've just edited them locally. If there is an interest in this, I can make a PR, when I find the time.

issue_title: Appropriately handling long running processes from causing Heartbeat session expirations
issue_body:
I have this consumer...

```
    def my_consumer(self):
      consumer = KafkaConsumer("topic", bootstrap_servers=self.kafka_server + ":" + str(self.kafka_port),
      enable_auto_commit=False,
      group_id="topic_group")
      for msg in consumer:
         dict = json.loads(msg.value.decode('UTF-8'))
         self.lots_of_work(dict["id"]) //this can last for a few seconds to 15 minutes
         consumer.commit()
```

I'll get this Warning message saying the heartbeat is timing out. This seems to indicate to me that session_timeout_ms is being hit and its being kicked out of the consumer group. Which seems to be the case because if I add session_timeout_ms=300000 into my KafkaConsumer() it doesn't time out as quickly. 

> 2018-05-09 10:19:06,607 - kafka.coordinator - WARNING - Marking the coordinator dead (node 0) for group steppart: Heartbeat session expired.

The  `self.lots_of_work(dict["id"])` is as small of unit as I can consume (it converts a single file with an external processes). This indicates that the actual heartbeat thread is timing out because my thread is possibly stopping the heartbeat thread from responding. Which seems to be the reason as indicated by this thread (https://github.com/dpkp/kafka-python/issues/1285). However, I'm not sure how to handle this to fix the issue. Is my assessment correct that the python GIL could be stopping the heartbeat thread from responding? Any suggestions on how to fix this?

Right now, a lot of ack type responses are canned, so people using `random_ack` get a sense that the bot just has a standard personality. Perhaps helga should have installable personalities, plugins that can define different types of responses. I think there's probably a few ways to do this:

1. A flatfile containing canned responses, one per line. They can be downloaded and configured to be used via settings
2. An installable plugin type module that provides canned responses
3. A simple list override for canned responses in settings

issue_title: order of information in connection string changes outcome
issue_body:
Please first make sure you have looked at:

* Documentation: https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/wiki
* Other issues

### Environment

To diagnose, we usually need to know the following, including version numbers.  On Windows, be
sure to specify 32-bit Python or 64-bit:

- Python: amd64
- pyodbc: 4.0.22
- OS: Windows 7 amdx64
- DB: access 2003 mdb
- driver: {Microsoft Access Driver (*.mdb, *.accdb)}

### Issue

Often it is easiest to describe your issue as "expected behavior" and "observed behavior".
This works
`>>> conn = pyodbc.connect('DSN=sample_mdb;DRIVER={Microsoft Access Driver (*.mdb, *.accdb)}')`

this fails

Ok I have 2 bug's to mention

This is the first one:

When downloading stories using a txt, depending on the last character of the input, the CLI might not download the correct story.
Specificaly if the last line of the input txt has the url:

> http://archiveofourown.org/works/4833515

the cli will instead try to download 

> http://archiveofourown.org/works/483351

Similarly if the last line is the url:

> https://www.fanfiction.net/s/11515678

it will try to download

> https://www.fanfiction.net/s/1151567

and fail since that story does not exist.

On the other hand urls like 

> https://www.fanfiction.net/s/11515678/1/This-Bites
> https://www.fanfiction.net/s/11515678/1/
> https://www.fanfiction.net/s/11515678/

and

> http://archiveofourown.org/works/4833515/chapters/11070479
> http://archiveofourown.org/works/4833515/

are fine because in those cases the url to be downloaded basically becomes

> https://www.fanfiction.net/s/11515678/1/This-Bite
> https://www.fanfiction.net/s/11515678/1
> https://www.fanfiction.net/s/11515678

and

> http://archiveofourown.org/works/4833515/chapters/1107047
> http://archiveofourown.org/works/4833515

and those redirect to the correct stories.

**Note:** The above obviously applies if the file does _not_ end in a newline. If there _is_ a newline then obviously that newline is the last character in the txt and it is that newline that is deleted

issue_title: Work around guess_type() bug with query strings.
issue_body:
In parse_media_file() a HTTP url gets fed through basename to get the "filename" and then passed to mimetypes.guess_type() to guess what the MIME type is based on the extension of the file. If your file has a query string guess_type() can't figure out the MIME type and returns None. This gets passed up to Twitter which ends up returning an error 'media type unrecognized'.

In some cases you can strip the query string/fragment with urlparse and hand that to python-twitter which will let it work properly. But what about URLs that *require* a query string to work? If you take it off yourself it'll guess the MIME type right but http_to_file() will fail as it's returning bad content. 

As a solution guess_type() should strip query strings before passing the URL to basename on line 218 in twitter_utils.py. The URL should be passed to http_to_file without change.

issue_title: field is not null but has no default
issue_body:
I have a migration with the following code:

```
def migrate(migrator, database, fake=False, **kwargs):
    """Write your migrations here."""
    MyModel = migrator.orm['my_table']
    type_new = pw.CharField(null=True, constraints=[pw.Check(
        "type_new in ('type1', 'type2', 'type3')")])
    migrator.add_fields(MyModel, type_new=type_new)
    migrator.sql('UPDATE my_table SET type_new=type')
    migrator.remove_fields(MyModel, 'type')
    migrator.rename_field(MyModel, 'type_new', 'type')
    migrator.add_not_null(MyModel, 'type')
```

And when appllying it I get following error:
```
ValueError: type_new is not null but has no default
````

I'm explicitly passing `null=True`, but peewee-migrate claims this field to be non-null, how it is possible? Is it some bug or am I missing something?

issue_title: Treat @-prefixed names in config declarations as meta information
Ease declaration of items in fixed order
issue_body:
User wants to be able to predict the order in which `config.iter_items` returns items within a section. That is impossible if config declaration is specified in a plain `dict`. Current workaround is to use an `OrderedDict`, but that makes it less readable:

```
config = Config(collections.OrderedDict([
    ('comment', Item()),
    ('description', ''),
    ('enabled', False),
    ('threads', Item(value=5)),
]))
```

**Better**:

```
config = Config([
    Item('comment'),
    ('description', ''),
    ('enabled', False),
    Item('threads', value=5),
])
```

issue_title: Feature request - friendlier API for calling operations
issue_body:
I've written a wrapper for working with pyswagger that makes it easier to call operations. Maybe someday you can include some of this to make calling a simple operation a bit easier. It has a lot of magic in it (the exec is ugly, I know), but it's the best way I could find to get functions with the right signature.

```
import functools
from pyswagger import SwaggerApp, SwaggerSecurity
from pyswagger.contrib.client.requests import Client


OP_FUNC = '''def op_func(client, op, override_url, {}):
    kwargs = locals()
    del kwargs['client']
    del kwargs['op']
    del kwargs['override_url']

    return client.request(
        op(**kwargs),
        opt={{'url_netloc': override_url}},
    )'''


class SwaggerWrapper(object):
    def __init__(self, swagger_path, oauth_token='', override_url=''):
        self.app = SwaggerApp._create_(swagger_path)

        if oauth_token:
            auth = SwaggerSecurity(self.app)
            auth.update_with('api_token', oauth_token)
            self.client = Client(auth)
        else:
            self.client = Client()

        self.override_url = override_url

        for op in self.app.op.values():
            exec(OP_FUNC.format(', '.join(p.name for p in op.parameters)))

            op_func = functools.partial(op_func, self.client, op, self.override_url)

            op_func.__name__ = op.operationId
            op_func.__doc__ = op.description
            setattr(self, op.operationId, op_func)
```

It's made just for my use case (single API token), but having the function signature be based off of the docs is helpful. It also has the docstring on it based off of the description from the swagger document (I  suppose I should also have it fall back to summary if description is missing).

In the example below I can just call c.post_import(some_data=data) and it returns the response. I find this a lot easier than calling the operation to get the request/response tuple, and then calling client.

```
In [1]: from swagger_wrapper import SwaggerWrapper
In [2]: c = SwaggerWrapper('http://swagger.dev.mysite.com/v1.yaml', 'mytoken123', 'my.local.instance:8003')

In [3]: c.post_import?
Type:            partial
String form:     <functools.partial object at 0x111d2be68>
File:            ~/.pyenv/versions/2.7.10/lib/python2.7/functools.py
Signature:       c.post_import(import_definition, property_id)
Docstring:
This is the description of the operation from the swagger doc.
Class docstring:
partial(func, *args, **keywords) - new function with partial application
of the given arguments and keywords.
```

Django 1.9 No longer supports django.utils.importlib import_module

https://github.com/jsocol/django-ratelimit/blob/f9b1e161d1389d8d83a51e6e97822d8475a73554/ratelimit/middleware.py#L2

```
django.utils.importlib is a compatibility library for when Python 2.6 was still supported. It has been obsolete since Django 1.7, which dropped support for Python 2.6, and is removed in 1.9 per the deprecation cycle.

Use Python's import_module function instead:

from importlib import import_module
The reason you can import it from django.utils.module_loading is that importlib.import_module is imported in that module, it is not because module_loading in any way defines the actual function.

Since django.utils.module_loading.import_module is not part of the public API, it can be removed at any time if it is no longer used - even in a minor version upgrade.
```
Source - https://stackoverflow.com/questions/32761566/django-1-9-importerror-for-import-module

In https://github.com/omab/python-social-auth/blob/master/social/storage/base.py,  the `CLEAN_USERNAME_REGEX` maybe error.  

```
CLEAN_USERNAME_REGEX = re.compile(r'[^\w.@+-_]+', re.UNICODE)
```

The `-` in `[]` means range of characters, if we want to match literal `-`, we can escape it or put it in the end.

The correct expression is

```
CLEAN_USERNAME_REGEX = re.compile(r'[^\w.@+_-]+', re.UNICODE)
```

or

`CLEAN_USERNAME_REGEX = re.compile(r'[^\w.@+\-_]+', re.UNICODE)`

ref: https://docs.python.org/3.4/library/re.html#regular-expression-syntax

issue_title: Namespace param to include breaks URLs output by jsreverse
issue_body:
Imagine I have a base `urls.py`:

```python
urlpatterns = patterns(
    '',

    url(r'^(?P<project_slug>[a-z0-9-_]{1,256})/signup/',
        include('myapp.signup.urls',
                namespace='signup',
                app_name='signup'),),
)
```

The URLs inside of `myapp.signup.urls` are defined as:

```python
urlpatterns = patterns(
    '',

    url(r'^create/?$',
        view=SignupConfigCreateView.as_view(),
        name='create'),
)
```

The result (relevant part only) of the JS is:

```
url_patterns = [
                [
                    'signup:create', ['(?P<project_slug>[a-z0-9-_]{1,256})/signup/create', []]
                ],
        ];
```

This is not correct. 

If I leave out the namespace param in the include call as such:

```python
urlpatterns = patterns(
    '',

    url(r'^(?P<project_slug>[a-z0-9-_]{1,256})/signup/',
        include('myapp.signup.urls',
                app_name='signup'),),
)
```

the result JS is correct:

```
url_patterns = [
                [
                    'create', ['%(project_slug)s/signup/create', ['project_slug']]
                ]
        ];
```

Just a minor note, but as you don't support 2.6 anymore it would be nice if you could remove it from the classifiers from `setup.py`. Otherwise the pypi page gives the impression you are still supporting it.

class MessageHandler():

	def __init__(self):
		self.last_restart = time.time()
		self.bot = telebot.TeleBot('INSERT TOKEN HERE')
		self.continue_flag = False
		self.first = True

class MessageHandlerThread(Thread):

	def __init__(self, message_handler):

	def stop(self):

	def run(self):

	def idle_time(self):

if __name__ == '__main__':

issue_title: Set DDF_IGNORE_FIELDS or ignore_fields argument using wild cards
issue_body:
Hi.

It would be nice to set `DDF_IGNORE_FIELDS` or `ignore_fields` argument using wild cards.

You could want to ignore a set of fields in one or more models.

By example, when I start using this app on my unit tests, I always get `AttributeError` on polymorphic models. This exception is documented on [django-polymorphic documentation](https://github.com/chrisglass/django_polymorphic/blob/master/docs/admin.rst#polymorphic-inlines). 

To workaorund this, I had to set all fields that ends with "_ptr" to be ignored.

It would be nice to be able to set `DDF_IGNORE_FIELDS = ['*_ptr']`.

Just one suggestion.

Best regards,
Roger

Hi,

I was planning on contributing the following updates to the project:

- Rewrite the bundled storage backend to internally provide and use `check_save` and `check_delete` methods that could be overridden per storage backend instead of overriding the whole `check_status` backend. This would not be a breaking change and would 
- Add a bundled S3 storage backend checker for AWS health checking compatibility, for example `health_check_storage_s3`, which utilizes the S3BotoStorage interface from the django-storages. This is a very widely used package in AWS and Heroku which would be nice to support here as well. It is officially recommended by Heroku;
- Upgrade README from Markdown to reStructuredText for PyPI documentation compatibility (this is the preferred format for Python packages and is supported by GitHub and many other VCS services out-of-the-box.

I thought to ask about the plan before starting the work as to avoid unnecessary changes that could be rejected in PRs.

issue_title: [CoreML Converter] Directly rely on Coreml's pooling
issue_body:
Converter's currently uses [custom code](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/tools/coreml/converter/_add_pooling.py) to support pooling layer as at the time of coding, Coremltools didn't support padding types apart from valid.

The latest version of coremltools does support other padding types for pooling layer. See [here](https://apple.github.io/coremltools/generated/coremltools.models.neural_network.html#coremltools.models.neural_network.NeuralNetworkBuilder.add_pooling).

So now we can remove the custom code and rely on coreml's pooling layer completely.

issue_title: (Question) Slow calculation and mulit thread issues (pyhton)
issue_body:
Hi,

for learning reasons, I implement the stochastic gradient descent by myself with mxnet.
Basically I do in my learning loop the following steps:

1. Select a sample from the learning data by random
2. Copy it to the executor memory
3. do forward calculation
4. calculate head gradient
5. do backward calculation
6. adapt weights of executor

I iterate this code around 50000 times and it takes around 15 seconds on my computer.
15 seconds does not sound much, however if I need to search 50 different arguments for the best fit with 10-fold-crossvalidation, then we talk about around 2 hours of training time. And this is bad.

I guess the internal learning algorithms from the library are much faster, but by using them there is only little I can learn. 
I also notices, that it really does not matter how many samples are calculated per iteration. If I calculate for validation 200 samples in the same loop, does not increase the learning time much, as long as I copy the data just once to the test-executor-memory. So setting up the memory for the train executor seems to be costly here, not the calculation of the executor. 

Does anyone has an idea how to speed up my learning? 

I also tried to learn different arguments (learning-rate, regularization rate) on different threads at the same time. Here every thread has its own training and test executors and writes only local data. So they should not share any resources. However I got race condition errors and the threads slows down it calculation enormously when running in parallel. So I guess even by using different executors, they have some kind of shared resource by mxnet?

I would be happy if someone can give me some hints about multi-threading with mxnet.

issue_title: This DAG crashes my scheduler very quickly (with explanation)
issue_body:
## Environment

- OS: Debian Wheezy (via [docker-airflow](https://github.com/puckel/docker-airflow))
- [Configuration](https://gist.github.com/boxysean/5b5d73f4fc3541d63c4b#file-airflow-cfg) (trimmed)
- python 2.7.9, Airflow 1.6.1 from pypi, [my project's requirements.txt](https://gist.github.com/boxysean/5b5d73f4fc3541d63c4b#file-requirements-txt) (trimmed)

## How to replicate and explanation

[This DAG](https://gist.github.com/boxysean/5b5d73f4fc3541d63c4b#file-dag_that_crashes_scheduler-py) crashes my scheduler very quickly when manually (externally) triggered. I'm going to guess it will crash for everyone running Airflow 1.6.1 and up to master.

I find my DAGs start to be scheduled only after manually / externally triggering them. (Not sure why.) The manual trigger generates this DagRun:

    <DagRun crash_dag @ 2015-12-17 16:28:34: test, externally triggered: True>

Then the subsequent scheduler heartbeats generates new scheduled DagRuns one at a time (one for each of the four previous minutes):

    <DagRun crash_dag @ 2015-12-17 16:24:34: scheduled__2015-12-17T16:24:34, externally triggered: False>
    <DagRun crash_dag @ 2015-12-17 16:25:34: scheduled__2015-12-17T16:25:34, externally triggered: False>
    <DagRun crash_dag @ 2015-12-17 16:26:34: scheduled__2015-12-17T16:26:34, externally triggered: False>
    <DagRun crash_dag @ 2015-12-17 16:27:34: scheduled__2015-12-17T16:27:34, externally triggered: False>

One minute passes, then it tries to schedule the next DagRun, but it crashes with this exception:

    2015-12-17 16:29:35,097 - root - ERROR - (_mysql_exceptions.IntegrityError) (1062, "Duplicate entry 'crash_dag-2015-12-17 16:28:34' for key 'dag_id'") [SQL: u'INSERT INTO dag_run (dag_id, execution_date, state, run_id, external_trigger, conf) VALUES (%s, %s, %s, %s, %s, %s)'] [parameters: ('crash_dag', datetime.datetime(2015, 12, 17, 16, 28, 34), u'running', u'scheduled__2015-12-17T16:28:34', 0, None)]
    Traceback (most recent call last):
       File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/airflow/jobs.py", line 608, in _execute
         self.schedule_dag(dag)
       File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/airflow/jobs.py", line 398, in schedule_dag
         session.commit()
       File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/sqlalchemy/orm/session.py", line 801, in commit
         self.transaction.commit()
    <snip>

The reason is my Airflow database (tested on both MySQL and Postgres) has a unique key definition in the `dag_run` table on `(dag_id, execution_date)`. Indeed, the DagRun attempting to be scheduled with ID `scheduled__2015-12-17T16:28:34` is colliding with my DagRun with ID `test`.

After this collision and exception, the scheduler remains alive, and for each scheduler heartbeat, I get the following SQLA rollback message, preventing all future scheduling:

    2015-12-17 16:29:35,115 - root - ERROR - This Session's transaction has been rolled back due to a previous exception during flush. To begin a new transaction with this Session, first issue Session.rollback(). Original exception was: (_mysql_exceptions.IntegrityError) (1062, "Duplicate entry 'crash_dag-2015-12-17 16:28:34' for key 'dag_id'") [SQL: u'INSERT INTO dag_run (dag_id, execution_date, state, run_id, external_trigger, conf) VALUES (%s, %s, %s, %s, %s, %s)'] [parameters: ('crash_dag', datetime.datetime(2015, 12, 17, 16, 28, 34), u'running', u'scheduled__2015-12-17T16:28:34', 0, None)]
     Traceback (most recent call last):
       File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/airflow/jobs.py", line 618, in _execute
         self.import_errors(dagbag)
       File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/airflow/jobs.py", line 340, in import_errors
         session.query(models.ImportError).delete()
       File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/sqlalchemy/orm/query.py", line 2883, in delete
         delete_op.exec_()
    <snip>

Looking into the Airflow codebase, I see that [the DagRun model has a single unique index](https://github.com/airbnb/airflow/blob/master/airflow/models.py#L2721-L2723) that is *not* the unique index causing this issue. There is, however, [a migration that adds the unique DagRun index](https://github.com/airbnb/airflow/blob/master/airflow/migrations/versions/1b38cef5b76e_add_dagrun.py#L29) causing this issue.

## How to fix

I'm going to try to work-around by manually deleting the unique constraint on the `dag_run.(dag_id, execution_date)` index. I'm guessing it will work okay for now.

I think the root cause is an inconsistency between the DagRun model and the list of migrations that needs to be resolved in such a way there isn't index collisions.

issue_title: Get token with credentials in Authorization header
issue_body:
Hi,

I am integrating mozilla-django-oidc with [Hydra](https://github.com/ory/hydra/). I have set up an authorization/consent server and I am able to initiate the OAuth flow using the  `mozilla_django_oidc.auth.OIDCAuthenticationBackend` at my application. 

However, at the point that `OIDCAuthenticationBackend` wants to obtain the token, I get a 400 Client Error in Django, with Hydra logging `HTTP authorization header missing or invalid: The request is missing a required parameter, includes an invalid parameter value, includes a parameter more than once, or is otherwise malformed`

The problem is documented [here](https://github.com/ory/hydra/issues/174#issuecomment-235325777). According to the Hydra developers, the client_id and client_secret should be provided in the Authorization header instead of [POST parameters](https://github.com/mozilla/mozilla-django-oidc/blob/7d186f9d03afe20c2a1d5e3219c1857a6894324a/mozilla_django_oidc/auth.py#L114). 

Right now, I don't see any other way than reimplementing/copying the OIDCAuthenticationBackend.authenticate() method in my custom backend. However, It would be very nice if I could only extend/hook/plug/configure how the client_id and client_secrets are passed to the remote token endpoint.

Thanking you in advance,
Wouter

Hey guys!

When inheriting from a task class that has a `task_namespace`, imho it would be pythonic that the inheriting class has the same `task_namespace` (if not set otherwise). However, this does not seem to be the case. Is this behavior intended by design?

### Possible solution

Due to the way the task register meta class implements the `_default_namespace` fallback, the proper namespace is only set when it's contained in the `class_dict`: ([link](https://github.com/spotify/luigi/blob/master/luigi/task_register.py#L67))

```python
class Register(abc.ABCMeta):

    def __new__(metacls, classname, bases, classdict):
        if "task_namespace" not in classdict:
            classdict["task_namespace"] = metacls._default_namespace

        cls = super(Register, metacls).__new__(metacls, classname, bases, classdict)
        metacls._reg.append(cls)
```

The fix would be quite simple:

```python
class Register(abc.ABCMeta):

    def __new__(metacls, classname, bases, classdict):
        cls = super(Register, metacls).__new__(metacls, classname, bases, classdict)

        if not getattr(cls, "task_namespace", None):
            cls.task_namespace = metacls._default_namespace

        metacls._reg.append(cls)
```

issue_title: Allow VpcConfig parameter to be empty in sagemaker.create_training_job()
issue_body:
Hello,

In boto3 1.7.19, the _VpcConfig_ parameter in _sagemaker.create_training_job()_ cannot be set to an empty dictionary. This causes code duplication when you want to retrain an existing job.

You have to do something like:

```python
job = sm.describe_training_job(TrainingJobName=name)

if 'VpcConfig' in job:
        resp = sm.create_training_job(
            TrainingJobName=job['TrainingJobName'], AlgorithmSpecification=job['AlgorithmSpecification'], RoleArn=job['RoleArn'],
            InputDataConfig=job['InputDataConfig'], OutputDataConfig=job['OutputDataConfig'],
            ResourceConfig=job['ResourceConfig'], StoppingCondition=job['StoppingCondition'],
            HyperParameters=job['HyperParameters'] if 'HyperParameters' in job else {},
            VpcConfig=job['VpcConfig'],
            Tags=job['Tags'] if 'Tags' in job else [])
    else:

        resp = sm.create_training_job(
            TrainingJobName=job['TrainingJobName'],    AlgorithmSpecification=job['AlgorithmSpecification'], RoleArn=job['RoleArn'],
            InputDataConfig=job['InputDataConfig'], OutputDataConfig=job['OutputDataConfig'],
            ResourceConfig=job['ResourceConfig'], StoppingCondition=job['StoppingCondition'],
            HyperParameters=job['HyperParameters'] if 'HyperParameters' in job else {},
            Tags=job['Tags'] if 'Tags' in job else [])
```

It would be quite simpler if we could do:

```python
job = sm.describe_training_job(TrainingJobName=name)

resp = sm.create_training_job(
            TrainingJobName=job['TrainingJobName'],    AlgorithmSpecification=job['AlgorithmSpecification'], RoleArn=job['RoleArn'],
            InputDataConfig=job['InputDataConfig'], OutputDataConfig=job['OutputDataConfig'],
            ResourceConfig=job['ResourceConfig'], StoppingCondition=job['StoppingCondition'],
            HyperParameters=job['HyperParameters'] if 'HyperParameters' in job else {},
            VpcConfig=job['VpcConfig'] if 'VpcConfig' in job else {},
            Tags=job['Tags'] if 'Tags' in job else [])
```

Thanks.

issue_title: Lazy initialization to speed up startup time and performance on Raspberry PI
issue_body:
I've got a Spotify library with a bunch of thousands of artists and tracks.

That means that mopidy spends a lot of time (~ 10/15 minutes) to pre-load and populate the artists/tracks hierarchies (Rasberry Pi 3 running Arch), and even when it's done every basic operation times out.

How hard would it be to implement some kind of "lazy fetch" mechanism? Or even entirely skipping the artists/tracks population as an option. I don't mind to just play my Spotify playlists on my RPi and do a regular search if I want something more, as long as it gets at least usable.

issue_title: Pipenv fails to honor environment markers, e.g. python_version
issue_body:
##### Issue description

Pipenv does not honor [environment markers](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0508/#environment-markers) in requirements.txt, or at least not `python_version`.

Example that is parsed incorrectly:

```
wsgiref==0.1.2;python_version<'3.0'
```

##### Expected result

wsgiref would be excluded from Pipfile because the Python version is 3 and requirements.txt said `python_version<'3.0'`.

##### Actual result

wsgiref is included in Pipfile.

##### Steps to replicate

Note: the same result is obtained whether or not `--three` is used and regardless of the whitespace in requirements.txt.

```
$ echo "wsgiref==0.1.2;python_version<'3.0'" > requirements.txt
$ pipenv install -r requirements.txt

Pipfile: /home/mark/tmp/pipenv-test/Pipfile
Using /usr/bin/python3 (3.6.5) to create virtualenv...
[... snip - see "Console output" below ...]
$ cat Pipfile
[[source]]
url = "https://pypi.org/simple"
verify_ssl = true
name = "pypi"

[packages]
wsgiref = "==0.1.2"

[dev-packages]

[requires]
python_version = "3.6"

issue_title: Remove redis from the framework requirements
issue_body:
Redis shouldn't be a framework requirement but the application requirement because an developer could choose to use file based session or even don't use session at all.

It is necessary remove redis from the requirement list and generate a requirements.txt with the redis requirement on it.

The example in the [doc](http://klein.readthedocs.io/en/latest/examples/twistd.html) works well with Twisted v16, but not with Twisted v17

issue_title: safeDump crashes with obscure "unknown reason" if JSON contains undefined
issue_body:
For example:

```js
yaml.safeDump({ foo: undefined })
```

throws

```
(unknown reason)

      at writeNode (node_modules/js-yaml/lib/js-yaml/dumper.js:731:13)
      at writeBlockMapping (node_modules/js-yaml/lib/js-yaml/dumper.js:610:10)
      at writeNode (node_modules/js-yaml/lib/js-yaml/dumper.js:703:9)
      at dump (node_modules/js-yaml/lib/js-yaml/dumper.js:792:7)
      at Object.safeDump (node_modules/js-yaml/lib/js-yaml/dumper.js:798:10)
      ...
```

It's not clear what _should_ happen, but any of these seem like better alternatives:
- Treat the key as absent if `undefined` is found as a value in an object
- Treat it the same as `null`
- Make the error message more descriptive: `"Encountered undefined value in JSON"` or somesuch

issue_title: Comments and escaping in plain style
issue_body:
I'm not sure if you're aiming for strict compliance with the YAML spec, but by my reading of the spec ("The plain (unquoted) style has no identifying indicators and provides no form of escaping...Plain scalars must never contain the “: ” and “ #” character combinations."), this should be illegal:

```
- foo:
    my \# commented
    string
```

Yet JS-YAML treats the \ as an escape. 

Interestingly, it doesn't allow `my \: colon`, yet accepts any character after a colon, including #: "

```
- foo:
    my :# non-commented
    colon
```

The build process used to have requirements scattered across many scripts in travis. 
This was probably due to the fact that PyPi didn't have wheels and thus some optimization was done around that.
There seems to have been an attempt at cleaning this up a while back, but the build process is still needlessly complicated.

Many of these are small changes to files, but combined, it can be confusing to understand what happens. I'll try to split them up as different PRs.

 * [x] 0. Ensure that Travis actually fails when the test script fails. 
    -  [x] Master  #3170. 
    -  [x] 0.14.x #3169 

**Everything below needs to be rebased onto 0. to ensure that a new build is actually triggered.**
 * [ ] 4. Move the cythonize script outside of the `skimage` directory. PR #3158.
 * [x] 1. Remove stray pip commands in travis install that don't use a requirements file. PR  #3227
 * [x] 2. Pin all requirements files and not just default.txt  PR #3227
 * [ ] 5. Use `pip install .` to install the package as opposed to calling the setup.py script directly.
    * Since we don't have a wheelhouse anymore, we can ask pip to install all the dependencies listed in the `setup.py` file itself.
    * Will have to use `pytest --pyargs skimage` as opposed to `pytest skimage` since scikit-image isn't installed in development mode anymore.
        * [ ] We install only build dependencies before the build.
 * [x] 6. Consistent Cython versioning: should all require `0.23.4`: PR #3171
 * [x] 7. Improve `build_versions.py` such that it is not hard coded. PR #3211
     * ~~Remove the tools/build_versions.sh script. This functionality is provided by `pip list` and breaks things as we add or remove dependencies.~~ @stefanv made good arguments to keep this scritpt PR #3181
 * [ ] 8. Remove matplotlib as a core dependency: WIP #3129
 * [x] 9. Prefer imageio over PIL (though PIL is still a dependency for tiffle): WIP #3126
 * [x] 10. Reduce the verbosity on the builds as travis is having a hard time keeping up. PR #3182
    * [x] Decrease Python messages
    * [x] run `pytest` without `-v`

Many of these fixes are in #3159, but that PR got too big to handle.

## Description
I am currently working on a project that requires in-depth understanding of one of the modules available in scikit-image. In that regard I require a source(preferably a paper) for the algorithms being used in skimage.transform.PeicewiseAffineTransform.estimate in this file below.
[_geometric.py](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/master/skimage/transform/_geometric.py)

issue_title: utils: deprecate invenio.utils.redis in favor of invenio.ext.cache
issue_body:
Currently redis host must be set both with ``CACHE_REDIS_URL`` (invenio.ext.cache) and ``CFG_REDIS_HOSTS`` (invenio.utils.redis). This is confusing especially when redis is not running on localhost (which is the default value).

* ``CFG_REDIS_HOSTS`` comes from Invenio legacy code, and uses Nydus package with consistent hashing to spread load over several caches.
* ``CACHE_REDIS_URL`` uses Flask-Cache and can be configured with many different cache backends.

Following has to be done:
- [x] Deprecate use of ``CFG_REDIS_HOSTS`` in favor of invenio.ext.cache
- [ ] Add support for using Nydus with consistent hashing in Flask-Cache.

The cover template is the one being used on e..g the login/signup pages.

- The template should extend from [OAUTH2SERVER_COVER_TEMPLATE instead of OAUTH2SERVER_BASE_TEMPLATE](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-oauth2server/blob/master/invenio_oauth2server/templates/invenio_oauth2server/authorize.html#L20)
- OAUTH2SERVER_COVER_TEMPLATE should be added to [ext.py](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-oauth2server/blob/master/invenio_oauth2server/ext.py#L159-L162) (following the [base template pattern](https://invenio-theme.readthedocs.io/en/latest/usage.html#base-template-pattern))

It seems like there was some sort of unlogged problem building the window wheels for the last release. @mgedmin would you mind restarting this build: https://ci.appveyor.com/project/mgedmin/btrees/build/1.0.60
Running the tests against persistent 4.2.3 leads to test-only failures with `TypeError` stating ``Cache values must be persistent objects.``.

issue_title: Optimized version of InterfaceBase doesn't use interface's __adapt__
issue_body:
In https://bugs.launchpad.net/zope.interface/+bug/98488, Ben Artin reported:

> According to zope.interface documentation, the `__call__` of an interface should use `__adapt__` in the interface to attempt to adapt the object. This is not so with the optimized C version of `InterfaceBase`:

    from zope.interface import Interface
    class Test(Interface):
        pass
    def adapt(*args, **kwargs):
        return 17
    from new import instancemethod
    Test.__adapt__ = instancemethod(adapt, Test)
    print Test.__adapt__(1) # prints 17
    Test(1) # Generates an exception

> This is not how `InterfaceBasePy.__call__` behaves -- it uses `interface.__adapt__`, which can be customized for each interface. This discrepancy between `InterfaceBasePy` and the C-optimized `InterfaceBase` should be eliminated; I believe that the behavior of `InterfaceBase` should change to match that of `InterfaceBasePy`.

@ctheune followed up:

> `__adapt__` is not documented in IInterface. However, the tests say that we do this and respect PEP 246 behaviour.

I am using the native models.UUIDField as my primary key in my Custom User model (which also uses email address as the USERNAME_FIELD.)  

The default implementation calls `user_id = user.pk`, which returns a UUID Instance instead of the actual identifying string.

Now I can see that I should probably override `JWT_PAYLOAD_HANDLER` with a function that ignores the `user_id` key, but in trying I keep getting random errors like "you need to set your SECRET_KEY" even though it is set. 

So my question is: how best to override the payload handler?  Where should that function live?  It refers to the conf.settings, but then the function is also supposed to live (or be imported into) the settings file itself?  Doesn't that cause a circular dependency?  

This might be more a django issue than a DRF-JWT issue, but I'm truly lost...

Look at method _notify in [AndroidNotification](https://github.com/kivy/plyer/blob/master/plyer/platforms/android/notification.py) class: 

    def _notify(self, **kwargs):
        icon = getattr(Drawable, kwargs.get('icon_android', 'icon'))
        noti = NotificationBuilder(activity)
        noti.setContentTitle(AndroidString(
            kwargs.get('title').encode('utf-8')))
        noti.setContentText(AndroidString(
            kwargs.get('message').encode('utf-8')))
        noti.setSmallIcon(icon)
        noti.setAutoCancel(True)

        if SDK_INT >= 16:
            noti = noti.build()
        else:
            noti = noti.getNotification()

        self._get_notification_service().notify(0, noti)

I have problems with encoding in this code.  [Demo app](https://github.com/Hukuta/kivy-plyer-issue-175) to demonstrate problem. 
Also a have opened ticket https://github.com/kivy/plyer/issues/175  11 days ago.

issue_title: Check presence of main.py during build time
issue_body:
Now it is possible to create an APK without a valid Python entry point. In this case the application just quickly flashes, then closes.

If the developer makes a mistake with packaging and main.py doesn't end up in the APK the application startup does not give any meaningful error message. For novice users it might be difficult to spot the error from adb logs.

issue_title: Buildozer doesn't work with multiple first-class directories
issue_body:
I have an existing project which can build. I've added kivymd for theming and stuff. I wish to vendor kivymd appropriately as I'm changing some of its code for my own use, so I place the kivymd directory in the same folder as main.py and my own first-class directory (name of my project).

Setting source.include_patterns to kivymd or similar (relative) does not work. The resulting apk (using android_new) doesn't have my kivymd directory. I can add kivymd's gitlab link in requirements, but then I lose all the changes I've done myself.

Workaround seems to be to host my own fork on gitlab, but it'd be easier to do it from files which are right there next to my own source. How is this supposed to work?

issue_title: New projects have 64-byte master keys, pycrypto supports only 16/24/32-byte AES
issue_body:
Generating scoped keys is breaking on newer projects, as the master keys are now 64-byte. They seemed to have changed from 32-byte keys in the past few months. I created a project a month or two ago that has a 32-byte key, and year+ old projects have 32-byte keys. Only the newer one from a week or two ago has 64-byte.

``` python
scoped_key = scoped_keys.encrypt(master_key, {
  "allowed_operations": ["read"],
  "filters": filters
})
```

Exception being thrown:
`ValueError: AES key must be either 16, 24, or 32 bytes long` from pycrypto.

My `keen` module is the lastest `0.3.20`, and `pycrypto` is the latest `2.6.1`.

issue_title: autoconf: Include statements should be in CPPFLAGS, not CFLAGS
issue_body:
When updating brotli from an older version, build may fail with ```error: ‘BROTLI_DECODER_PARAM_LARGE_WINDOW’ undeclared``` because the Include statements for the brotli headers use the installed location first when invoking the compiler. This can be fixed by moving the include statement for brotli's own header files from AM_CFLAGS to AM_CPPFLAGS.
```
diff --git a/src/ext/brotli-1.0.3/Makefile.in b/src/ext/brotli-1.0.3/Makefile.in
index 75dac8d617..44068c093e 100644
--- brotli-1.0.3/Makefile.in
+++ brotli-1.0.3/Makefile.in
@@ -495,7 +495,7 @@ BROTLI_INCLUDE = \
 
 brotliincludedir = $(includedir)/brotli
 brotliinclude_HEADERS = $(BROTLI_INCLUDE)
-AM_CFLAGS = -I$(top_srcdir)/c/include
+AM_CPPFLAGS = -I$(top_srcdir)/c/include
 brotli_SOURCES = $(BROTLI_CLI_C)
 brotli_LDADD = libbrotlidec.la libbrotlienc.la libbrotlicommon.la -lm
 #brotli_LDFLAGS = -static
```

issue_title: bindings of variables are not carried into FILTERs in (NOT) EXISTS clauses
issue_body:
My understanding is that variable bindings carry into FILTER constructs.  However, this sometime does not work in rdflib.  I have a short example that shows this, with a FILTER inside a FILTER EXISTS.  My belief is that the binding of ?this from the outermost portion should be visible in the innermost portion, meaning that the ?this = ?valu will only filter out solutions where the two variables have different bindings.  However, all solutions appear to be filtered out, which would happen if the ?this binding(s) from the outermost portion are not making their way into the innermost portion.

This is very similar to the example in SPARQL 1.1 Query Language section 8.3.3.

[bug2-rdflib.txt](https://github.com/RDFLib/rdflib/files/184221/bug2-rdflib.txt)

issue_title: Improve test suite coverage of wfirst module
issue_body:
The wfirst module [currently](https://codecov.io/gh/GalSim-developers/GalSim/tree/20142beef4301bfd37f1cd035b7f9c16d1d3c3f4/galsim/wfirst) has a coverage percentage of about 85%, which isn't too bad, but could definitely be improved.  The detectors and bandpass files in particular have a lot of missing test coverage.  This issue is to rectify that.

The biggest culprits are:
1. The `allDetectorEffects` function is apparently not run at all in the test suite.
2. It looks like the `kwargs` option to `getBandpasses` is never run.  Do we need that?  It's apparently not so useful, since it was never used in the tests, so maybe just get rid of that option and let users run thin and/or truncate themselves if they so desire.
3. The `high_accuracy` and non-`approximate_struts` options to `getPSF` are not well covered.  IIRC, they are really slow and are thus skipped in nosetests runs.  We might need to use the [mock](https://docs.python.org/dev/library/unittest.mock.html) module to edit things like `galsim.wfirst.pupil_plane_file` for the purpose of testing to get tests that run in a reasonable amount of time but still touch all the relevant code.

issue_title: Do not crash when asked for a parameter after end date
issue_body:
As someone who use openfisca as a backend of a simulation application,
My application doesn't crash overnight without me having done anything,
Because we just passed the end date of one legislative parameter

----------------

The changes introduced with the fuzzy parameters are a catastrophe for anyone using openfisca in prod. It introduces a major production risk : if we pass the end date of a parameter we use, then the application cannot do any calculation anymore.

The PNDS (institutional re-user) application was down for some time because of this, as well as some of our testing instances.

Openfisca should offer a "production" mode where it is flexible and extrapolates parameters values, and a "strict" mode where it crashed when a value is missing if it is necessary.

issue_title: Verify JIRA server SSL not passed
issue_body:
https://github.com/pycontribs/jira/blob/0c386cbf7958fd49a81df128527748fe35a6383d/jira/client.py#L223

When trying to connect to a jira server using a self-signed cert, I am unable to pass in the verify keyword argument to avoid cert errors. All the mechanism is in place, but the __init__ function does not allow

When trying to connect to a jira server using a self-signed cert, I am unable to pass in the verify keyword argument.

`JIRA_C = jira.JIRA(server='https://jira.example.com', verify=False, basic_auth=('jirauser', 'changme'))`

**Describe the bug**

The `jira.resources.Resource.update` method contains a `time.sleep(4)` near the end of its method, and I think this introduces a lot of performance issues:

https://github.com/pycontribs/jira/blob/master/jira/resources.py#L294-L296

```python
        # TODO(ssbarnea): compare loaded data in order to verify if resource was updated indeed
        # we had random test failures (probably) due to caching
        time.sleep(4)
```

* For non-async code, this adds 4 seconds to every resource updated. For example, if I wish to update every issue that matches some query, that could take a significant amount of time since it adds 4*N seconds. For say, 10 issues, that would add 40 seconds to the total time to update.
* This seems to apply even when using the experimental async feature? (I'm not sure whether the issue's fields/state is reliable after starting an async update, but that's another behavior)

It looks like this sleep was introduced in order to help make CI tests pass. However, it also looks like it negatively impacts normal use of the library.


**Expected behavior**

I would expect that this delay should either be configurable, or only enabled for CI testing (although this may be problematic since that would mean that the code behaves differently in a significant way between CI and normal uses).

** Additional Context **

For my use, I've written an app that responds to webhooks by doing some queries and then updating some related issues and fields. However, if there are more than a handful of issues to update, this sometimes takes a long time, causing my app to run into default timeouts (eg, gunicorn defaults to a 30 second timeout). I can up gunicorn's timeout to 60 seconds, but there are still 60 second-per-request limits since I'm using Google AppEngine for ease of deployment.

issue_title: Local vs Lambda string encoding is different
issue_body:
I'm trying to upload an image via `multipart/form-data` through chalice. From my understanding, this should be possible and handled by `cgi`. The issue seems to be in the difference between how chalice handles the input sent when running locally or when running on a lambda. 

For example, when running locally the `app.current_request._body` is encoded as `str`. This seemed to be fine and I was able to locally write the file. I swapped the file writing for boto s3 methods and then started running into issue. Now the `app.current_request._body` is encoded as `unicode`. Encoded this way, my current `cgi` handling doesn't work. Below is an example with some output in terms of the type and how I've been processing and saving the file for different environments. 

```python
import cgi
import uuid
from io import BytesIO
import boto3

from botocore.exceptions import ClientError
from chalice import Chalice

app = Chalice(app_name='s3-uploader')
app.debug = True


@app.route('/upload', methods=['POST'], cors=True, content_types=['multipart/form-data'])
def upload():
    bucket_name = 'some bucket name'
    request_body = app.current_request._body

    # locally using `chalice local` request_body isinstance `str`
    # when running on lambda request_body isinstance `unicode`
    if isinstance(request_body, str):
        app.log.debug("ordinary string")
    elif isinstance(request_body, unicode):
        app.log.debug("unicode string")
    else:
        app.log.debug("not a string")

    content_type_obj = app.current_request.to_dict()['headers']['content-type']
    content_type, property_dict = cgi.parse_header(content_type_obj)
    form_data = cgi.parse_multipart(BytesIO(request_body), property_dict)

    ext = 'png'
    extracted_image = form_data['file'][0]

    if len(form_data['file']) > 0:
        app.log.debug('got a file!')
        temp_name = uuid.uuid4()
        fullname = '{}.{}'.format(temp_name, ext)
        try:
            # Trying to eventually do this
            s3 = boto3.resource('s3')
            s3.Bucket(bucket_name).put_object(Key=fullname, Body=extracted_image)

            # This can save locally but obviously doesn't make sense on the lambda
            with open('/path/to/output/file/test.png', 'w') as f:
                f.write(extracted_image)

        except ClientError as e:
            app.log.debug("ERROR: %s" % e)

        return {'status': 'success'}

    else:
        return {'status': 'error parsing file'}
```

Is this difference intended? Or am I using an incorrect chalice method? Or am I processing the data incorrectly?

Thanks!

issue_title: Passing scale to Optimizer changes gradient clipping threshold
issue_body:
Trainers accept an initial learning and a decay parameter. To control the learning rate dynamically without using the built-in decay, one can pass a ```scale``` parameter to the ```trainer.update(scale)``` function. Unfortunately, this directly affects gradient clipping:

From https://github.com/clab/dynet/blob/master/dynet/training.cc#L68:

```
if (scale * gg > clip_threshold) {                              
   ++clips;                                                      
   ++clips_since_status;                                         
   gscale = clip_threshold / (scale * gg);                       
}  
```                                                             

Using ```trainer.update(scale)``` to manage the learning rate, ```clip_threshold``` is effectively divided by ```scale```. Though there might be a reason for this, I was surprised by it. To get the same results as I would with TensorFlow, I have to compensante for this by calling ```trainer.set_clip_threshold(clip_threshold * scale)``` before calling ```update(scale)```.

issue_title: feature request: better api for arbitrary keyword argument pass to query ?
issue_body:
from this example https://googlecloudplatform.github.io/google-cloud-python/stable/bigquery-usage.html#querying-data-asynchronous

```python
>>> dataset = client.dataset('dataset_name')
>>> table = dataset.table(name='person_ages')
>>> job = client.run_async_query('fullname-age-query-job', query)
>>> job.destination = table
>>> job.write_disposition= 'WRITE_TRUNCATE'
>>> job.name
'fullname-age-query-job'
>>> job.job_type
'query'
>>> job.created
None
>>> job.state
None
```

how about a better api by allowing arbitrary keyword list to be appended on the insert job POST request
https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/reference/rest/v2/jobs the keyword `destinationTable` and `writeDisposition` here are from rest api reference, this way can keep `google-cloud-python` library to be a thinner layer, just gives user the best flexibility

```python
>>> job = client.run_async_query(query,
          destinationTable={'datasetId': 'dataset_name', 'tableId': 'person_ages'},
          writeDisposition='WRITE_TRUNCATE',
          )
```

in python it can be implemented as dict as last argument:

```python
def run_async_query(query, **kwargs):
    # populate from kwargs to be on the `configuration.query` in the post request body,
    # it could optionally have jobId
```

Btw, from #3209 I am not puting a `job_name` / `jobid` field because I don't think that's necessary, the `run_async_query` shouldn't require user to provide a `jobid`, in the rest api insert job request, if jobid is absent, server side would generate a unique id

issue_title: Pubsub message getting wrong attribute for publishTime
issue_body:
According the [REST docs](https://cloud.google.com/pubsub/docs/reference/rest/v1/PubsubMessage), a `PubsubMessage` has the field `publishTime`

In [message.py](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/master/pubsub/google/cloud/pubsub/message.py), `from_api_repr` is getting the field `publishTimestamp` below:
```
instance._service_timestamp = api_repr.get('publishTimestamp')
```

The current tests are self-confirming of this issue as they simply set up the api_repr with `publishTimestamp`

A quick fix seems to adjust the following:
**message.py**
```python
    @classmethod
    def from_api_repr(cls, api_repr):
        """Factory:  construct message from API representation.

        :type api_repr: dict or None
        :param api_repr: The API representation of the message

        :rtype: :class:`Message`
        :returns: The message created from the response.
        """
        data = base64.b64decode(api_repr.get('data', b''))
        instance = cls(
            data=data, message_id=api_repr['messageId'],
            attributes=api_repr.get('attributes'))
        instance._service_timestamp = api_repr.get('publishTime')
        return instance
```

**test_message.py**
```python
    def test_from_api_repr_no_attributes(self):
        from base64 import b64encode as b64
        DATA = b'DEADBEEF'
        B64_DATA = b64(DATA)
        MESSAGE_ID = '12345'
        TIMESTAMP = '2016-03-18-19:38:22.001393427Z'
        api_repr = {
            'data': B64_DATA,
            'messageId': MESSAGE_ID,
            'publishTime': TIMESTAMP,
        }
        message = self._getTargetClass().from_api_repr(api_repr)
        self.assertEqual(message.data, DATA)
        self.assertEqual(message.message_id, MESSAGE_ID)
        self.assertEqual(message.attributes, {})
        self.assertEqual(message.service_timestamp, TIMESTAMP)

    def test_from_api_repr_w_attributes(self):
        from base64 import b64encode as b64
        DATA = b'DEADBEEF'
        B64_DATA = b64(DATA)
        MESSAGE_ID = '12345'
        ATTRS = {'a': 'b'}
        TIMESTAMP = '2016-03-18-19:38:22.001393427Z'
        api_repr = {
            'data': B64_DATA,
            'messageId': MESSAGE_ID,
            'publishTime': TIMESTAMP,
            'attributes': ATTRS,
        }
        message = self._getTargetClass().from_api_repr(api_repr)
        self.assertEqual(message.data, DATA)
        self.assertEqual(message.message_id, MESSAGE_ID)
        self.assertEqual(message.service_timestamp, TIMESTAMP)
        self.assertEqual(message.attributes, ATTRS)
```

I don't currently have a contributor license signed, but will work on that.  In the meantime, hoping that someone can pick this up.

issue_title: Swap steps 2, 3 and 4 in setup wizard (suggestion)
issue_labels: [{'id': 492821376, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/learningequality/kolibri/labels/DEV:%20frontend', 'name': 'DEV: frontend', 'color': 'DFDFDF', 'default': False}, {'id': 732420792, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/learningequality/kolibri/labels/TAG:%20needs%20design', 'name': 'TAG: needs design', 'color': 'c1e23b', 'default': False}, {'id': 492816719, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/learningequality/kolibri/labels/TASK:%20ux%20update', 'name': 'TASK: ux update', 'color': '72486f', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
### Observed behavior

Setup wizard is:

1. **Please select the default language for Kolibri**
1. **Name your Facility**
1. **Create your Admin account**
1. **Choose a Facility setup**

### Expected behavior

1. **Please select the default language for Kolibri**
1. **Choose a Facility setup**
1. **Name your Facility**
1. **Create your Admin account**

Because the type of facility chosen may guide the kinds of questions and help texts in the remainder of the wizard, we could move the question to the beginning.

Immediately, moving **Choose a Facility setup** to the beginning means that when we ask users to **Name your Facility**, the choice of name is more easily understood because the wizard has exposed roughly which types of facilities Kolibri has to do with.

In the longer run, we can adapt the help text of **Name your Facility** to for instance "Name your school" or the help text below it.

Finally, moving **Create your Admin account** to Step 4 will mean that the admin can log in fresh after choosing her password.

### User-facing consequences

Mainly it's the perception of what a facility is I worry about.

issue_title: channel resource URLs no longer include channel
issue_body:
### Observed behavior

Our resource URLs now look like this:

* `/topics/t/:id`
* `/topics/c/:id`
* `/recommended/:id`

### Expected behavior

URLs could include the channel ID also, e.g.:

* `/topics/:channel/t/:id`
* `/topics/:channel/c/:id`
* `/recommended/:channel/c/:id`

### User-facing consequences

URLs no longer contain a channel ID, which might make it harder to report issues with content items by sharing the URL.

It's also not clear what happens in the UI if the same content resource exists in multiple channels.

### Context

* Kolibri version: 0.6.x

issue_body:
The following bundle import fails with the error `charm cs:trusty/keystone-25 cannot be deployed to machine with different series precise` I don't believe the host should matter because the unit will be deployed into a kvm on the host machine.

  "0":

  "1": 
    series: trusty
    constraints: "arch=amd64 cpu-cores=1 cpu-power=100 mem=1740 root-disk=8192"

    series: precise

What exactly is referred to as a "residual" throughout pyamg? For example [here](http://pyamg.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pyamg.html#pyamg.multilevel.multilevel_solver.solve) in the `residuals` list?

I ask because I have a linear system, for which this code:
```python
A, x0, b = load_system(prefix + "4")

residuals = []

ml = pyamg.aggregation.smoothed_aggregation_solver(A, coarse_solver="lu")
x = ml.solve(b, x0, tol=1e-8, accel="gmres", residuals=residuals)

print("residuals: ", np.array(residuals))
print("initial residual: ", np.linalg.norm(b - A*x0))
print("final residual:   ", np.linalg.norm(b - A*x))

```
gives this output:
```
residuals:  [  1.90742365e+03   2.46237095e+02   7.37640869e+01   4.24415154e+01
   2.26020896e+01   1.01097677e+01   6.54103003e+00   2.90056919e+00
   1.21443941e+00   2.94870147e-01   9.30577374e-02   3.87827062e-02
   1.21317950e-02   5.13556765e-03   1.23089031e-03   3.07927290e-04
   9.55081888e-05   2.94020487e-05   8.99521969e-06]
initial residual:  169.162191899
final residual:    7.33922070348e-07
```
Note especially, that the first item in the `residuals` array is `1.90742365e+03` vs. my custom `169.162191899`. For the final residue it's `8.99521969e-06` vs. `7.33922070348e-07`. But we have `8.99521969e-06 / 1.90742365e+03 == 4.715900261591074e-09` and `7.33922070348e-07 / 169.162191899 == 4.338570351383219e-09` which is pretty close and both are less than the specified tolerance as expected.

But with a different system, I get the following output:
```
residuals:  [  7.46798679e+03   6.12041588e+02   2.23696785e+02   4.86324879e+01
   2.12922568e+01   1.72794230e+01   1.34075286e+01   7.11332293e+00
   2.42608324e+00   1.14781268e+00   4.44119832e-01   2.42504621e-01
   1.57739497e-01   4.83938585e-02   2.04185224e-02   9.14724819e-03
   3.50229029e-03   1.03405520e-03   3.19180567e-04   1.20335344e-04
   4.69263331e-05]
initial residual:  139.669408523
final residual:    4.0189140226e-06
```
Here the ratio of the pyamg residuals is `4.69263331e-05 / 7.46798679e+03 == 6.28366578832687e-09`, which is less than the specified tolerance. But the ratio of my residuals is `4.0189140226e-06 / 139.669408523 == 2.8774475850509438e-08`, which is __greater than__ the specified tolerance, so the approximation is not good enough.

The systems are 44684 x 44684 with 442680 nonzeros and 178648 x 178648 with 1778160 nonzeros, respectively, which are still pretty small problems. Please let me know what you need to be able to track this down, I might be able to even provide the linear system data.

issue_title: undefined symbol: PyFPE_jbuf on stock Ubuntu 17.10
issue_body:
Trying to import after a successful install gives:

```sh
    from cytoolz import (
  File "~/venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/cytoolz/__init__.py", line 3, in <module>
    from .functoolz import *
ImportError:~/venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/cytoolz/functoolz.cpython-36m-x86_64-linux-gnu.so: undefined symbol: PyFPE_jbuf
```

It appears that cytoolz was built against a cpython with `--with-fpectl` on, which causes the above error when run on a cpython that is built without the flag. Ubuntu had the flag in the stock python in 17.04, then removed the flag in 17.10

Here is the unresolved issue with Python devs discussing how to handle it: https://bugs.python.org/issue29137

Is it possible to release a cytoolz that is built against a cpython without the flag? I don't see the fpectl module imported in pytoolz. (I'm pretty clueless in the area, so pardon my ignorance).

Some other relevant discussions, which mostly suggest building your own cpython (or cytoolz) locally as the only known workaround:
- https://stackoverflow.com/a/47703373/8412986
- https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/8415
- https://github.com/ethereum/web3.py/issues/554
